# Analysis of Office Hours Attended and Rating of Class Difficulty

## Conclusion

The data we analyzed supports the suggestion that office hours should be incentivized more. The majority of students went to 0 office hours, and the majority of difficulty responses for the course were rated a 4 or 5 out of 7. In our displot graph, there were more dark squares and thus more responses in the region where 0-1 office hour visits were linked to a difficulty rating of 4 or 5. This shows that although many people found the course difficult, they did not attend office hours.
An incentive, such as extra credit points on exercises, could drive more students to try out office hours and recognize how helpful they are. Office hours could help students explore their critical thinking skills while understanding the concepts of the exercises better, a domino effect for future assignments that can steer them away from ai useage.
To explore further, we could ask students if going to office hours changed their difficulty rankings. For example, if attending office hours in the beginning of the semester was helpful, the students may have rated their difficulty rankings much lower, explaining the outliers we see in the displot. However, incentivizing office hours more doesn't guarantee a big shift in the amount of students who go. With busy schedules, setting aside extra time outside of class to focus more on the course material can be challenging. This trade-off of going to more office hours could lead to worse grades in other classes for students. Additionally, more students going to office hours could overwhelm the TA's who assist them. This would lead to either more TA's being needed to help or longer wait times for aid in office hours.
